text: Here you can see that a complete fissure is found even if in this case as almost every S1 segmentectomy the fissure doesn't need to be dissected.
After an initial exploration of the pleural cavity we routinely start the procedure with radical lymphadenectomy.
We begin the dissection at the inferior pulmonary ligament and we resect station 9 lymph nodes when found.
The dissection then continues upwards along the posterior pleura, reaching the posterior aspect of the right inferior bronchus.
Here the station 7 lymph nodes are resected until identification of the contralateral bronchus.
The upper lobe, bronchus and the interbronchial carina between the upper and the lower bronchi are dissected to obtain a better exposure and allow an easier bronchial isolation on a second moment.
A common anatomical reference is a station 11 lymph node, usually found at the interbronchial carina.
The posterior dissection stops at the lower aspect of the azygus vein.
Then, we change position and carry on over the superior aspect of the ilum, dissecting the pleura over the area of the azygus vein.
The tissues over the pulmonary artery and the bronchus are dissected.
The station to our lymph nodes are resected in the triangle formed by the superior vena cava, the vagus nerve, and the esgus vein.
The station 4 air lymph nodes are resected deeply to the posterior surface of the esagus vein.
The tissues over the pulmonary artery and the bronchus are dissected.
This dissection also allows the subsequent isolation of the bronchus and the artery.
It is essential to identify the correct dissection plane of the sheath around the artery to achieve adequate exposure.
Here some station 12 and 11 lymph nodes can be found and, as in this case, they are usually sent for frozen section.
In case of ossitive N1 lymph nodes, a conversion to lobectomy is indicated.
Here the A1A arterial branch is identified.
After anatomy confirmation, the artery is isolated and stapled.
We use the SureForm robotic staplers for our procedures.
The dissection continues in order to isolate the bronchus.
To verify the correct bronchus isolation, we sometimes perform a reventilation test.
Here you can see a correct ventilation of the ventral and the dorsal segments.
However, for the intersegmental plane identification, this test is not always precise as the indiocyanin green, which we will use in the video.
Now we are looking for the second arterial branch, the A1b.
After verifying the anatomy, we decide to resect the A1B branch after venous resection.
So we change position to approach the hilum anteriorly.
The pleura and the tissues over the veins are dissected.
Here as said at the beginning of the video, we avoided to staple the additional V1B branch.
After completing the resection of all bronchovascular elements, we proceed with intravenous indiocyanin green injection to identify the intersegmental plane.
Once identified, the intersegmental plane is marked with bipolar energy, then the stapling begins.
Careful is made to include the bronchovascular stumps with the resected segment
and the surgical specimen is extracted with an endobag.

We usually place an hemostatic dressing on the baritis space and in the subcarinal space and sometimes, as in this case, we also position an aerostatic dressing on the parenchymal staple line or in the areas at risk for air leak.
A 24 French chest tube is positioned posteriorly and a current ventilation of the remaining lung is ensured.
